output:
Context: Both Kafka and RabbitMQ are messaging systems used in distributed architectures.
Options: Kafka (streaming platform), RabbitMQ (message broker).
Trade-offs: Kafka is designed for high-throughput, distributed streaming and log-based systems, while RabbitMQ is better for complex routing and low-latency message delivery.
Recommendation: Use Kafka for event streaming and large-scale pipelines, RabbitMQ for task queues and traditional messaging.
Failure cases: Kafka may be overkill for simple queues, RabbitMQ may struggle with very high throughput.

Context: Both are API design approaches.
Options: REST (resource-based), GraphQL (query-based).
Trade-offs: REST is simpler and widely supported, while GraphQL allows flexible data fetching but adds complexity.
Recommendation: Use REST for simple APIs, GraphQL when clients need flexible queries.
Failure cases: GraphQL can cause performance issues if poorly designed, REST can lead to over-fetching.

Context: Architectural styles for backend systems.
Options: Monolith (single codebase), Microservices (distributed services).
Trade-offs: Monoliths are simpler to build and deploy, microservices offer scalability but add operational complexity.
Recommendation: Start with a monolith, move to microservices as system complexity grows.
Failure cases: Microservices can fail due to poor service boundaries, monoliths can become hard to scale.

Context: Database paradigms.
Options: SQL (relational), NoSQL (non-relational).
Trade-offs: SQL provides strong consistency and structured schema, NoSQL offers flexibility and scalability.
Recommendation: Use SQL for structured data, NoSQL for unstructured or high-scale applications.
Failure cases: NoSQL may lack consistency, SQL may not scale easily without tuning.

Context: Virtualization technologies.
Options: Docker (containers), Virtual Machines.
Trade-offs: Containers are lightweight and fast, VMs provide stronger isolation but are heavier.
Recommendation: Use Docker for microservices and deployment pipelines, VMs for full OS isolation.
Failure cases: Containers may face security issues, VMs can be resource-heavy.

Context: Data processing approaches.
Options: Batch processing, Stream processing.
Trade-offs: Batch is simpler and cost-effective, stream processing provides real-time insights but is complex.
Recommendation: Use batch for historical data, streaming for real-time applications.
Failure cases: Streaming systems can be hard to maintain, batch systems may introduce latency.

Context: Deep learning models for image tasks.
Options: CNN, Vision Transformer.
Trade-offs: CNNs work well with smaller datasets, transformers require large datasets but scale better.
Recommendation: Use CNN for limited data, transformers for large-scale training.
Failure cases: Transformers may overfit on small data, CNNs may struggle with global context.

Context: Sequence modeling architectures.
Options: LSTM, Transformer.
Trade-offs: LSTMs handle sequential data well but are slower, transformers parallelize better and scale.
Recommendation: Use transformers for modern NLP tasks.
Failure cases: Transformers require large compute, LSTMs may struggle with long dependencies.

Context: Python web frameworks.
Options: Flask, FastAPI.
Trade-offs: Flask is simple and flexible, FastAPI provides better performance and automatic validation.
Recommendation: Use FastAPI for APIs, Flask for simple apps.
Failure cases: Flask can become unstructured, FastAPI may feel complex initially.

Context: Execution models in programming.
Options: Synchronous, Asynchronous.
Trade-offs: Synchronous is easier to understand, asynchronous improves performance for IO-bound tasks.
Recommendation: Use async for high concurrency applications.
Failure cases: Async code can be hard to debug, sync code may block performance.

Context: Scaling strategies.
Options: Horizontal scaling, Vertical scaling.
Trade-offs: Horizontal scaling increases system complexity, vertical scaling is limited by hardware.
Recommendation: Use horizontal scaling for distributed systems.
Failure cases: Horizontal scaling needs load balancing, vertical scaling hits hardware limits.

Context: ML pipeline design.
Options: Feature engineering, End-to-end learning.
Trade-offs: Feature engineering requires domain knowledge, end-to-end learning requires more data.
Recommendation: Use feature engineering for smaller datasets.
Failure cases: End-to-end models may overfit, feature engineering may miss patterns.

Context: Infrastructure choices.
Options: On-premise, Cloud.
Trade-offs: On-premise offers control, cloud offers scalability and flexibility.
Recommendation: Use cloud for scalability, on-premise for sensitive data.
Failure cases: Cloud costs can grow, on-premise lacks flexibility.

Context: Data processing locations.
Options: Edge computing, Cloud computing.
Trade-offs: Edge reduces latency, cloud provides scalability.
Recommendation: Use edge for real-time processing.
Failure cases: Edge devices are limited, cloud introduces latency.

Context: Machine learning paradigms.
Options: Supervised learning, Unsupervised learning.
Trade-offs: Supervised requires labeled data, unsupervised works without labels but is less precise.
Recommendation: Use supervised when labels are available.
Failure cases: Supervised models need data, unsupervised models may be hard to evaluate.
